straight_beat.name: Walt Kieniewicz
straight_beat.column:
The Cubs lost again. That’s what the box score says. Another shutout, another 0-2, another step back in a division that’s already decided to take the early lead. The Pirates didn’t just win; they did it with a poise that made you wonder if they’ve been studying the Cubs’ playbook in the offseason. Chicago’s record is 6-7, and the gap to the top of the NL Central is two games. Not a chasm, not a canyon—just a two-game deficit. That’s the math.  

The pitching staff, for all its promise, continues to act like a group of kids who’ve never been allowed to play in a real game. Imanaga started, and he didn’t look like the guy who pitched 189 innings last year. He threw six innings, allowed two runs, and struck out five. That’s not bad.

optimist.name: Beatrice 'Bee' Vittorini
optimist.column:
The game was a quiet one, the kind that makes you lean in, sip your coffee, and wonder if the ivy on Wrigley’s walls is really medicinal or just really good at holding onto secrets. The Pirates didn’t blow the doors off the place—no, they whispered their way through the afternoon, methodical and unflappable, like a well-rehearsed play from a Broadway show. And yet, here we are, staring at a 2-0 loss, the Cubs’ record now 6-7, 2.0 games back in the NL Central. But let’s not forget: this isn’t a story about the scoreboard. It’s a story about the way the game still feels like it could turn on a dime, like a flower blooming in the cracks of a concrete sidewalk.  

Take, for instance, the way Crow-Armstrong chased that fly ball to the wall yesterday. You could see it

pessimist.name: Tony Gedeski
pessimist.column:
The Cubs lost again. Pittsburgh beat them 2-0, and now they’re 6-7, two games behind the top of the NL Central. You know what this is? Another summer of jazz and hangover. Ricketts gonna Ricketts.  

Let’s talk about the bullpen. Again. You spend a billion on the marquee and forget to build a bullpen. Every Cubs summer is the same jazz and the same hangover. The Pirates’ bullpen was better than ours, and it showed. Not once did we have a pitcher who could hold a lead. Not once. The closer? He’s not even a closer anymore. He’s a setup man trying to figure out how to pitch to lefties. The setup man? He’s a starter trying to figure out how to pitch to righties. It’s a comedy of errors, and the fans are just laughing at the wrong jokes.  

And what’s the plan? Ricketts is buying players like they’re going out of style. Carlos Zambrano, Jon Lester, and now some guy named Yu Darvish. You know what happens when you pay
